panelReasoning: Unanimous 4/4 for C (integrated circuit). EXTRACTION ERROR CONFIRMED: the source-archaeologist rendered 'exam noooow.pdf' p.30 and the distractor-analyst read the same text layer (page index 29, single column at x=72); both report the paper DOES print a key line, 'Ans. (b)', directly beneath the option block. The pipeline's keyProvenance 'none' and its quarantine reason ('the source PDF marks no key for this item') are therefore false - the extractor silently dropped the printed 'Ans.' line. Stem and options were extracted faithfully and in order, so no stem/option correction is needed. The recovered source key is NOT imported: (b) resistor is factually indefensible, a resistor being a single discrete passive component while 'chip' is the everyday nickname for a monolithic integrated circuit. Mis-join was tested and rejected: the page is single-column, the Ans line sits under its own options, and every neighbouring key on the page attaches correctly and is correct on the merits (storage-devices-EXCEPT -> (b) Printers, physically-touch -> (a) Hardware, keyboard -> (b) input); shifting keys by one breaks those pairings. No annotations exist on p.30 to override the print. The panel's derivation stands: 'chip'/'microchip'/'silicon chip' name the IC itself, so C. D (semiconductor) is the designed trap - it names the MATERIAL CLASS the die is fabricated from, not the device; A (transistor) and B (resistor) are components fabricated on the die, part-for-whole errors. Key derived, not imported; keyProvenance stays 'derived' because the source value was caught wrong.

Unanimous 4/4 for D (Modem), confidence 0.97-0.99 across the panel. EXTRACTION ERROR CONFIRMED: the source-archaeologist (p.32 rendered) and the distractor-analyst (page index 31, single column at x=72) both read a printed key line 'Ans. (b)' under the option block, so the pipeline's keyProvenance 'none' is wrong - the key was present in the paper and dropped in extraction. Stem and options match the paper exactly and in order, so no correction is applied to them. The recovered key is NOT imported: (b) = 'Blue cord' is self-evidently corrupt and is the cleanest proof in the bank that these printed Ans letters are unreliable. Mis-join ruled out: adjacent items on the same page key correctly and plausibly (hard disk -> (d) computer hardware, surgeons/robotics -> (a) robotics), so this is a bad source value, not a systematic off-by-one. Merits: the stem is a verbatim restatement of the definition of a modem (MOdulator-DEModulator) - it modulates outgoing digital bits onto an analog carrier for the telephone voice band and demodulates the incoming carrier back to digital. A (Red wire), B (Blue cord) and C (Tower) name no device class that performs signal conversion; 'tower' is a chassis form factor and the other two are colour-named cabling filler. Every judge found an overwhelming rejection argument against A/B/C and none against D.

Unanimous 4/4 for A (Optical disk). EXTRACTION ERROR CONFIRMED: the source-archaeologist (p.33 rendered) and the distractor-analyst (page index 32, single column at x=72) both read a printed 'Ans. (c)' beneath the options, so keyProvenance 'none' and the 'source marks no key' quarantine reason are false - the extractor dropped the printed key. Stem and options were extracted faithfully and in order, so neither is corrected. The recovered key is NOT imported: (c) 'Magnetic disk' is provably wrong. Two independent proofs. (1) Formal: options C 'Magnetic disk' and D 'Magnetic disc' are semantically identical - disk/disc is a spelling variant only - and a single-answer MCQ cannot have two identical correct options, so BOTH must be wrong and the printed key is corrupt regardless of domain knowledge. (2) Physical: a CD-ROM is a pre-pressed optical disc storing bits as pits and lands in a reflective layer, recovered purely by laser reflectance; there is no magnetic layer anywhere in the read or write path, which kills C and D, and kills B (magneto-optical) too, since MO media are rewritable, laser-heated above the Curie point and written by a magnetic head - B is the trap for anyone pattern-matching on the word 'optical'. Mis-join ruled out: single-column page, and the neighbouring keys attach correctly and are correct (monitor resolution -> (c) Dots per inch, storage-devices-EXCEPT -> (b) printers). ITEM DEFECT NOTED (three judges): the C/D duplicate-option pair is a genuine construction flaw carried in from the printed paper and should be repaired editorially; it does not threaten answerability or key uniqueness, since both duplicates are wrong for the same reason and A is the sole optical option. No flag emitted because no value in the Flag union covers duplicate options; the defect is recorded here in the panel reasoning.
